title: [Discussion] On the use of training on validation set
body:
Dear Organizers,

Thank you for organizing such a creative and open-ended competition. I really appreciate the spirit of the challenge, and I wanted to share one concern about the current evaluation setup.

I feel the evaluation budget may be too loose, since it is effectively the same as the training budget. **Under the current setup, I think people will likely converge on various forms of training directly on the validation set.** As the rules are written, as long as nothing larger than 16MB is carried into evaluation, it seems valid to bring in pretrained weights from training and then do fine-tuning, test-time adaptation, or even expand effective model capacity during evaluation. Since the challenge is defined around a 16MB artifact, a 10-minute budget on 8×H100s, and evaluation on the FineWeb validation set, this seems like a natural direction for participants to explore.  ￼

In my own experiment with 4 GPUs (RTX6000) for 20 minutes, which I think is roughly comparable to the 8×H100 10-minute setting, I started from the baseline and followed this path: train on training set → quantize to 8-bit (under 16MB) → take the quantized model back to full precision and fine-tune on the validation set for about 18 minutes → use the remaining 2 minutes for evaluation.
| Stage | Val bpb |
|---|---:|
| Baseline / train → eval | 1.2287 |
| After 8-bit quantization (Baseline provided) | 1.2348 |
| After validation-set fine-tuning | 1.0712 |
| PR #64 that optimizes arch/hyper-parameter with validation-set finetuning| 1.0149| 

This is a big leap compared to simply doing train → eval, but much less interesting as one is overfitting on eval.

I completely understand the intention of keeping the competition open-ended and creative. My concern is simply that the current setup may reward how much a method can overfit within the evaluation budget on the evaluation set itself, rather than other forms of innovation, such as architecture design or training algorithms. Also, ideally, a good test-time adaptation method should not be nearly as expensive as training.

I think it may be worth reflecting a bit more on how much the current setup encourages test-time adaptation tricks while also increasing the risk of overfitting to the evaluation set. Perhaps a separate track with a stricter evaluation-time resource limit (e.g. 10x or more less compute allowed during validation) could help encourage more innovation beyond overfitting the evaluation set, while still preserving the open-ended spirit of the competition.

Thank you again for organizing this challenge. I hope this feedback is helpful, and I would be very interested to hear your thoughts.
